nativeRange.habitat: Open, clear, alkaline waters of Lake Kutubu and the Soro River outflow; littoral zones with aquatic vegetation and submerged branches near the surface

decorPreferences: long open swimming corridor for schooling and color display
dense planting along sides and rear (Vallisneria, Java fern, Amazon sword)
floating plants to diffuse light and reduce stress
fine-leaved plants or spawning mops for egg scattering
driftwood or smooth rocks as visual breaks
dark substrate to enhance turquoise coloration

diet.notes: In the wild feeds on algae, zooplankton, small insects, and invertebrates at the surface and mid-water. In captivity readily accepts quality flake, small pellets, and frozen foods (daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworm, mysis). Regular variety and colour-enhancing foods (spirulina, astaxanthin-rich flake) intensify turquoise and blue iridescence. Feed 2–3 times daily in small amounts.

avoidWith: small nano fish that may be outcompeted or stressed
dwarf shrimp (fry and juveniles may be eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Melanotaenia lacustris; male in breeding coloration showing turquoise-blue flanks.

summary: The Turquoise Rainbowfish is a deep-bodied species endemic to Lake Kutubu, Papua New Guinea. Males develop brilliant turquoise-blue iridescence that intensifies during spawning. Unlike most rainbowfish, it requires hard, alkaline water. Listed as IUCN Vulnerable due to its extremely limited natural range.
careNotes: The Turquoise Rainbowfish (Melanotaenia lacustris) is a deep-bodied member of the family Melanotaeniidae whose males develop brilliant turquoise-to-blue iridescence that intensifies dramatically during courtship displays. A tight shoal of six or more in a long tank (120 cm or more) is needed to observe the full color-display competition between males. Unlike most rainbowfish that tolerate soft, slightly acidic to neutral water, M. lacustris is endemic to Lake Kutubu — a hard, alkaline lake in the Southern Highlands of Papua New Guinea — and requires pH 7.5–8.5 with moderate to hard water (gH 10–20, kH 6–12). Soft or acidic conditions tend to cause stress, pale coloration, and increased susceptibility to disease. Neutral to slightly hard tap water in many areas suits this species well. Temperature should be kept in the range 22–26°C, as excessively warm water is thought to shorten lifespan. The species is active in the mid-water column and upper reaches of the tank and benefits from a long aquarium with clear swimming lanes. Dense peripheral planting is appreciated but the central swim corridor should remain open. Excellent filtration and regular water changes (25–30% weekly) are important, as this lake species is adapted to clear, well-oxygenated water. Males may display mild fin-nipping among themselves; a ratio of two to three females per male generally helps reduce this behavior. This species is listed as IUCN Vulnerable due to its restricted endemic range (Lake Kutubu and its sole outflow), making captive-bred stock the preferred choice. See also: [boesemans-rainbowfish] (Melanotaenia boesemani) and [eastern-rainbowfish] (Melanotaenia splendida) — related species with similar husbandry but softer water tolerance.
breedingNotes: The Turquoise Rainbowfish is an egg-scatterer that spawns among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops, typically in the morning hours. Males display intensely to females, driving them into vegetation. Eggs are adhesive and typically hatch in 7–10 days at 24°C. Parents do not guard eggs and will readily consume them if left in the same tank, so moving spawning mops to a dedicated fry tank significantly improves yield. Fry are small at hatching and require infusoria or commercial liquid fry food initially, transitioning to baby brine shrimp nauplii after 5–7 days. Conditioning adults on live and frozen foods is generally advised to promote spawning. Breeding is considered moderately straightforward for a shoaling species once appropriate water parameters are established.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Melanotaenia lacustris
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Lake Kutubu, Papua New Guinea), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.5, hardness 10–20 dH, temperature 22–26°C), adult size (males to 11 cm), diet, tank requirements, and conservation status (IUCN Vulnerable).

additional.name: FishBase — Melanotaenia lacustris
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Melanotaeniidae, Atheriniformes), adult size range, native range confirmation (Lake Kutubu and Soro River, Southern Highlands, Papua New Guinea), temperature range, and IUCN status. Confirms alkaline pH preference and hard water habitat.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rainbowfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General rainbowfish husbandry guidance; tank size recommendations (4-foot+ tanks), group size (6+), diet (omnivore — flake, frozen, color-enhancing foods), and compatibility confirmed against trade experience.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Melanotaenia lacustris
additional.notes: Supplementary profile confirming alkaline water requirement, endemic Lake Kutubu range, and intermediate care level. Notes that color displays are maximized in groups of 6+ with males competing.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Melanotaenia lacustris
additional.notes: IUCN Vulnerable assessment (limited to Lake Kutubu and Soro River outflow). Confirms conservation significance and value of maintaining captive-bred stock.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawns by scattering adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops in morning light. Eggs typically hatch in 7–10 days at 24°C. Adults will eat eggs if not removed promptly. Fry require infusoria initially, then baby brine shrimp nauplii after 5–7 days. Conditioning adults on live and frozen foods is generally advised to encourage spawning.
